subject: Careers
big_idea: Reflecting on our preferences and skills helps us identify the steps we need to take to achieve our career goals.
learning_goals: I can identify what makes a goal stronger or weaker.
I can improve a vague goal by making it clearer and more realistic.
I can create one goal with a practical next-step plan.

content.subject: Careers
content.subtitle: What makes a goal useful instead of just sounding good?

scenario_lines.text: Goal A: Do better in school.
Goal B: Bring missing assignments up to date this week by finishing one after school each day.
Which goal would actually help someone move forward?

prompts: What still might get in the way?

slides.title: A goal is not a wish

rows.head: A plan
rows.body: The actions that help you move toward the goal.

steps: Start with the weak goal: Get healthier.
Make the scope realistic for right now.
Add one first step you could actually do.

items.rest: what support or adjustment would help?

rows.head: First step
rows.body: What can you do next, not someday?

rows.head: Obstacle + support
rows.body: What might get in the way, and how will you respond?

lines.text: Choose one goal you could actually work on.
Include: your goal, why it matters, one first step, one likely obstacle, and one support or adjustment.

content.instruction: Choose a school, activity, savings, habit, or personal-growth goal you are okay working on in class.

rows.text: it is clear enough to act on.
it includes a first step and an obstacle response.
adjusting a plan is part of goal setting, not failure.

goals: I can identify what makes a goal stronger or weaker.
I can improve a vague goal by making it clearer and more realistic.
I can create one goal with a practical next-step plan.

anchor: A strong goal is more than something you want. It helps you know what to do next.
In this lesson, you will compare weak and strong goals, improve a vague goal, and make a simple plan you could really use.
supported.tip: If your goal is too vague, ask: What could I actually do this week?

questions.q_text: Circle the stronger goal and explain why: (A) Do better in school. (B) Bring missing assignments up to date this week by finishing one after school each day.
Finish the checklist: A stronger goal has a clear ______, realistic ______, one first ______, and a plan for an obstacle or ______.
Make this weak goal stronger: Save money. Add how much, by when, and one first step.
Write one goal you could actually work on. Include why it matters, one obstacle, and one support or adjustment.

game.title: Extra-time goal fixer
lines: Use only after Q1–Q4 are finished.
1. Start with a weak goal: Get more organized.
4. Add one obstacle and one support.

self_check.headline: Did you choose the stronger goal for a practical reason?
Did you include the missing parts of a stronger goal?
Did you make the weak goal more specific?
Does your personal goal plan show what you can do next?

answer_key.criterion: Student identifies Goal B as stronger and gives a practical reason.
notes: Look for clearer target, time frame, or action step.
A one-sentence explanation is enough.

answer_key.criterion: Student completes the missing checklist ideas accurately enough.
notes: target, scope, step, support are expected
Accept close synonyms if the meaning fits.

answer_key.criterion: Student revises the weak goal into a more usable version.
notes: Look for amount/target, time frame, and first step.
It should remain realistic.

answer_key.criterion: Student includes a goal, reason, first step, obstacle, and support or adjustment.
notes: The goal should be workable, not just motivational.
Privacy level can vary.

anchor: Useful goals are clear enough to guide action. They do not rely on motivation alone.
A stronger goal usually includes a clear target, realistic scope, a reason it matters, one first step, and a plan for a likely obstacle.
In this lesson, you will compare goals, revise weak examples, and create a practical goal plan of your own.

questions.q_text: Compare Goal A and Goal B from class. Which goal is more useful, and why?
List five things that make a goal stronger or more usable.
Rewrite each weak goal so it becomes more usable: (a) Get healthier. (b) Save money.
Create one realistic goal with a reason, one first step, one likely obstacle, and one support or adjustment. Explain why your version is stronger than a wish.

game.title: Goal revision challenge
lines: Use only after Q1–Q4 are finished.
1. Pick a weak goal from class.
2. Improve the target and time frame.

self_check.headline: Did you explain what made the stronger goal more usable?
Did your checklist include both goal and planning features?
Did your revised goals become more specific and realistic?
Does your goal plan show what you can do next and how you will respond to a barrier?

answer_key.criterion: Student identifies the more useful goal and explains why.
notes: Goal B is stronger.
Look for clarity, realism, and/or action step reasoning.

answer_key.criterion: Student lists the main features of a strong goal.
notes: clear target
realistic scope
reason it matters
first step
obstacle/support

answer_key.criterion: Student revises both weak goals into more actionable versions.
notes: Revisions should show more than added wording.
They should make the goal more usable.

answer_key.criterion: Student builds a realistic plan and explains why it is stronger than a wish.
notes: Look for a real first step and an obstacle response.
This question supports rubric use.

anchor: Reflecting on our preferences and skills helps us identify the steps we need to take to achieve our career goals.
Goal setting is not only about deciding what you want. It is about designing a plan that can survive reality: time limits, setbacks, distractions, and revision.
In this lesson, evaluate what makes a goal useful, revise weak goals, and defend a practical plan using the Career Education Big Idea.

questions.q_text: Explain why a strong goal is more useful than a vague wish. Use one example.
Rewrite Get healthier and Save money into stronger goals. Then explain what improved in each one.
Choose one revised goal and explain one realistic obstacle, one support, and how you would revise the plan after a setback.
Using the Big Idea wording, argue why reflecting on preferences and skills should lead to concrete steps rather than only big intentions.

self_check.headline: Did you explain the difference between usefulness and inspiration?
Did you say what improved in each revised goal?
Does your obstacle/support response sound realistic?
Did you use the Big Idea meaningfully in your argument?

answer_key.criterion: Student explains that a strong goal is more useful because it guides action.
notes: A simple example should support the explanation.
The answer should contrast vague wanting with practical planning.

answer_key.criterion: Student revises both goals effectively and explains what improved.
notes: Look for stronger specificity and realism.
The explanation should identify the actual improvement.

answer_key.criterion: Student explains one realistic barrier, one support, and one revision-after-setback move.
notes: The plan should sound workable.
This question shows planning depth.

answer_key.criterion: Student uses the Big Idea wording to argue that reflection should lead to concrete steps.
notes: This is an argument, not just a summary.
Look for connection between preferences/skills and action.

rubrics.title: Q4 — Goal plan
rubrics.question: Create one realistic goal with a reason, one first step, one likely obstacle, and one support or adjustment. Explain why your version is stronger than a wish.

criteria.emerging: Goal is present but vague or missing major planning parts.
criteria.developing: Goal is somewhat usable but planning details are uneven or incomplete.
criteria.proficient: Goal is realistic and includes clear planning details, including a first step and obstacle response.
criteria.extending: Goal plan is precise, realistic, and shows strong practical thinking throughout.

criteria.emerging: Explains weakly or mostly repeats the question.
criteria.developing: Gives some explanation of why the goal is stronger than a wish.
criteria.proficient: Clearly explains why the plan is more usable than a vague wish.
criteria.extending: Gives a strong, convincing explanation that connects action, realism, and follow-through.

rubrics.title: Q4 — Big Idea argument
rubrics.question: Using the Big Idea wording, argue why reflecting on preferences and skills should lead to concrete steps rather than only big intentions.

criteria.emerging: References the Big Idea loosely or incompletely.
criteria.developing: Uses some of the Big Idea but not yet as a full support for the argument.
criteria.proficient: Uses the Big Idea accurately to support the argument.
criteria.extending: Uses the Big Idea precisely and integrates it smoothly into a strong argument.

criteria.emerging: Reasoning is limited or mostly general.
criteria.developing: Reasoning shows some connection between reflection and action.
criteria.proficient: Reasoning clearly argues that reflection should produce concrete next steps.
criteria.extending: Reasoning is well-developed, persuasive, and grounded in realistic planning.

target_understanding: Students understand that stronger goals are clear, realistic, and action-oriented.
Students understand that obstacles and revision are normal parts of planning.
Students can connect reflection on strengths/preferences to practical next steps.
observation_targets: ☐ Students can tell the difference between a wish and a usable goal
☐ Students include a real first step and an obstacle response in their planning

watch_for.misconception: Students think a goal is just something they want.
watch_for.redirect: What could you actually do next?

watch_for.misconception: Students think changing a plan means they failed.
watch_for.redirect: How could revising the plan help you keep moving instead of stopping?

forward_connection: Students can return to this planning structure in later career planning, project work, and pathway decisions.

timing.activity: Warm-up comparison of weak and stronger goals
Wish vs goal vs plan and stronger-goal checklist
Revise weak goals together and independently
Personal goal-plan writing and teacher conferencing

differentiation_notes: ● Supported: guided checklist completion, clearer prompts, and a more structured planner.
◆ Proficient: standard revise-and-plan tasks with moderate independence.
▲ Extending: stronger explanation, revision-after-setback thinking, and Big Idea argument.
teacher_notes: Keep the tone practical, not slogan-heavy.
Do not force private disclosure; students can choose a safe school, activity, or routine goal.
Push for usable planning language rather than vague motivation talk.
